def: The study of abnormalities in the physiologic functioning of living beings — the intersection of pathology (disease study) and physiology (normal function study).
The study of the causes or reasons for a disease or phenomenon.
A condition whose cause is unknown.
A condition resulting from an unintended or unwanted effect of medical treatment.
A factor that increases the probability of disease but where the causal link is less than certain.
A factor closely and definitively linked to a disease's development (e.g., a specific pathogen for an infectious disease).
The development or evolution of a disease, from initial stimulus to the full expression of its manifestations.
An objective manifestation of disease, observable/measurable by an examiner (e.g., vomiting).
A subjective abnormality reported only by the affected individual (e.g., nausea).
A constellation of signs and symptoms whose underlying etiology has not yet been determined.
The interval between exposure to an injurious agent and the first appearance of signs/symptoms.
The appearance of the first, often nonspecific, signs/symptoms indicating disease onset (e.g., headache, malaise).
The stage at which a disease reaches its full intensity, with signs/symptoms at their greatest severity.
A stage during which disease processes are established but the patient still functions normally — no symptoms yet perceived.
A relatively sudden increase in the severity of a disease or its signs/symptoms.
A decline/abatement in the severity of signs and symptoms; if permanent (often >5 years), the person is considered cured.
The stage of recovery after a disease, injury, or surgery.
A pathologic condition resulting from a prior disease (e.g., cardiac valve scarring after rheumatic fever).
A new, separate disease process arising secondarily because of changes produced by the original problem (e.g., bacterial pneumonia after a viral infection).
The degree to which a measurement reflects the true value of what it's meant to measure.
A test's ability to give the same result on repeated measurement — can be reliable yet still inaccurate.
The probability a test will be positive in someone who has the condition; low sensitivity = more false negatives.
The probability a test will be negative in someone who does NOT have the condition; low specificity = more false positives.
The probability disease is actually present given a positive test result.
The probability disease is actually absent given a negative test result.
A disease native to, and consistently present in, a specific local region.
A disease disseminated to many individuals at the same time within a region/population.
An epidemic that has spread worldwide across multiple countries/continents.
A normal adaptive physiologic response to an environmental change (e.g., increased RBC count at high altitude).
Preventing disease onset entirely, by reducing susceptibility or exposure (e.g., vaccination).
Early detection and management of disease in its preclinical/early clinical stages (e.g., screening).
Rehabilitative/supportive care in advanced disease or disability, aimed at restoring function.
The immune system recognizing "self" as foreign and mounting a tissue-damaging response — a failure of self-tolerance.
The immune system's learned ability to not attack the body's own cells; involves both central and peripheral mechanisms.
Elimination/suppression of self-reactive T and B cells during development in the thymus and bone marrow.
Backup mechanisms (clonal deletion, anergy, Treg suppression) controlling self-reactive lymphocytes that escaped central tolerance.
CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ cells that suppress autoreactive T and B cells via inhibitory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β) and CTLA-4.
A theory of autoimmunity where pathogen epitopes resemble self-antigens closely enough that the immune response against the pathogen also attacks self tissue.
Self-antigens normally isolated from immune surveillance (cornea, testicles, brain) become exposed after tissue damage and are attacked as foreign.
Recurrent infection/organ damage causes the immune system to develop multiple new autoantibodies over time.
A normal immune mechanism triggered inappropriately, excessively, or with undesirable effects; four types (I–IV).
The principal antibody mediating type I hypersensitivity; binds mast cell/basophil Fc receptors.
Release of preformed (histamine, heparin, enzymes) and newly-formed (leukotrienes, prostaglandins) mediators triggered by IgE-antigen cross-linking.
A life-threatening, systemic type I hypersensitivity reaction.
An immune deficiency not attributable to another cause — congenital or acquired (e.g., HIV/AIDS).
Immune impairment resulting from another disorder or treatment (chemotherapy, malnutrition, stress).
Severe combined immunodeficiency — genetic disorders causing absent/dysfunctional T cells (± B cells/NK cells), leaving both cellular and humoral immunity impaired.
A T-cell disorder from a hypoplastic/aplastic thymus due to a chromosome 22q11.2 deletion.
An X-linked disorder (WASp gene) causing B-cell dysfunction, microthrombocytopenia, and eczema.
The most common B-cell PID — lack of serum IgA; often asymptomatic but linked to autoimmune/allergic conditions.
A "circulating tumor" disseminated from the start, primarily involving blood and bone marrow.
A malignancy that localizes in lymph tissue, though often disseminated by diagnosis.
Malignant transformation of B-lymphocyte plasma cells, with a predilection for forming bone tumors.
Enlarged, nontender lymph nodes — a common finding in lymphoma and some leukemias.
Overproduction of one or more functional, normal-appearing blood cell types from a multipotent stem cell (e.g., CML, PV, ET).
Neoplastic cells that are morphologically/functionally abnormal (unlike myeloproliferative disease) — poor prognosis.
The balanced 9;22 translocation forming the BCR/ABL fusion gene — characteristic of CML.
The fusion gene/oncoprotein from the Philadelphia chromosome; a functional enzyme driving proliferation and blocking apoptosis.
Drug class binding the BCR/ABL domain, dramatically improving CML survival.
An immature precursor cell; blast percentage in marrow/blood defines leukemia phase/type (e.g., ≥20% blasts = AML).
An AML subtype with t(15;17)/PML-RARα fusion; most curable AML subtype, especially with ATRA.
A drug that releases PML-RARα's inhibitory block on myeloid differentiation — used specifically in APL.
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia / small lymphocytic lymphoma — the same disease process, distinguished only by location (blood/marrow vs. nodes/spleen).
Malignant transformation of CLL/SLL into a more aggressive B-cell lymphoma — poor prognosis.
An immature lymphoid precursor cell characteristic of ALL.
Light-chain antibody fragments found in urine in multiple myeloma; can cause kidney damage.
The malignant B-cell-derived diagnostic cell of Hodgkin lymphoma — often a minority of the actual tumor cellularity.
The staging system for Hodgkin lymphoma based on node location/number and presence of systemic ("B") symptoms.
The most numerous blood cell; biconcave, non-nucleated disk that transports O2 via hemoglobin; lifespan 80–120 days.
The oxygen-carrying protein comprising ~90% of RBC dry weight — 2 α-chains + 2 β-chains, each with an iron-containing heme group.
The RBC's ability to change shape (torpedo-like) to squeeze through capillaries narrower than its own resting diameter.
The developmental process from pluripotential stem cells to mature blood cells; occurs in bone marrow in adults.
An immature RBC that has lost its nucleus but still contains organelle remnants; matures into an erythrocyte in 24–48 hours; normal count ~1% of total RBCs.
The kidney hormone that stimulates RBC production in response to tissue hypoxia.
The plasma protein that transports dietary iron to transferrin receptors on the RBC membrane.
The intracellular iron storage protein.
Two α + two γ chains; more efficient O2 carrier at low O2 tension; replaced by hemoglobin A within ~120 days after birth.
Two α + two β chains; ~97% of adult hemoglobin.
Hemoglobin reversibly bound to oxygen.
The PO2 at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated — the standard reference point for assessing hemoglobin's oxygen-releasing ability.
The shift in the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve caused by changes in PCO2/H+ that enhances O2 uptake in the lungs and release in the tissues.
Unloading of O2 in tissues facilitates the loading of CO2 onto hemoglobin.
Chloride diffuses into the RBC to balance the charge as bicarbonate diffuses out, maintaining electrical neutrality during CO2 transport.
Hemoglobin directly bound to CO2 — an important acid-base buffer.
A deficit of red cells causing reduced oxygen-carrying capacity and tissue hypoxia.
An excess of red cells causing increased blood viscosity and volume.
Simultaneous deficiency of red cells, white cells, and platelets — hallmark of aplastic anemia.
A stem cell disorder with reduced hematopoietic marrow tissue and fatty marrow replacement, causing pancytopenia.
A myeloproliferative neoplasm from stem cell transformation, causing overproduction of RBCs, WBCs, and platelets together.
Arrest of bleeding / prevention of blood loss after vessel injury.
The initial response to vessel injury — vasoconstriction plus platelet plug formation.
Formation of a fibrin clot (coagulation) to reinforce the platelet plug.
The final stage — platelet plug, fibrin strands, and trapped RBCs compress into a firm clot (~1 hour).
Platelet release of α granules (thrombospondin, fibrinogen, vWF, factors V/VIII) and dense bodies (ADP, ATP, serotonin) after activation.
A potent platelet aggregation agonist formed from arachidonic acid; blocked by aspirin/COX inhibitors.
Platelet surface receptor that binds fibrinogen to link platelets together during aggregation.
The active enzyme (from prothrombin) that converts fibrinogen to fibrin and activates factors V, VIII, and XIII.
The insoluble protein threads (from fibrinogen) that form the structural scaffold of a clot.
Coagulation activated by blood contact with damaged endothelium/negatively charged surfaces; assessed by aPTT.
Coagulation activated by tissue factor release after vascular/tissue trauma; assessed by PT/INR.
Where intrinsic and extrinsic pathways converge — factor Xa converts prothrombin to thrombin.
A natural anticoagulant that binds and inactivates free thrombin; its activity is enhanced by heparin.
Vitamin K-dependent anticoagulant proteins; protein C inactivates factors V/VIII, protein S assists it and triggers fibrinolysis.
Clot dissolution via plasmin, which digests fibrin/fibrinogen and produces fibrin split (degradation) products.
Flat, pinpoint, nonblanching red/purple spots from capillary hemorrhage — classic in vascular/platelet disorders.
Petechiae occurring in groups or patches.
A bruise — blood escaping into tissue; if raised, called a hematoma.
Bleeding into a joint, with swelling and pain — the hallmark of hemophilia.
A dilated, tortuous small vessel that blanches with pressure and bleeds easily with minor trauma.
Prothrombin time / international normalized ratio — assesses the extrinsic pathway.
Activated partial thromboplastin time — assesses the intrinsic pathway.
Low platelet count, a common cause of generalized bleeding.
An acquired, immune-mediated cause of thrombocytopenia; classified as newly diagnosed, persistent, or chronic.
X-linked recessive factor VIII deficiency — the classic form, ~85% of hemophilia cases.
X-linked recessive factor IX deficiency — less common than hemophilia A.
A disorder of von Willebrand factor (vWF), causing both platelet dysfunction and reduced factor VIII stability.
An acquired syndrome of simultaneous widespread clotting AND bleeding, from consumption of clotting factors/platelets.
The principles governing blood flow — pressure, resistance, velocity, laminar/turbulent flow, wall tension, compliance.
The formula describing how vessel length, radius, and blood viscosity determine resistance to flow.
Q = P/R — blood flow equals the pressure difference divided by resistance.
Resistance peripheral to the heart and lungs; primarily determined by arteriolar tone.
Distance traveled per unit time (cm/sec); varies inversely with total cross-sectional area.
Streamlined blood flow in layers, with central blood moving fastest (parabolic profile).
Disrupted, crosswise flow — audible as a bruit, palpable as a thrill; promotes thrombus formation.
Wall tension = distending pressure × vessel radius; explains why large-radius, high-pressure vessels (aneurysms) are prone to rupture.
The pressure (~20 mmHg) below which a vessel can no longer be held open and flow ceases.
The continuous baseline level of vascular smooth muscle contraction from basal vasomotor center discharge.
An organ/tissue's intrinsic ability to maintain relatively constant blood flow despite changes in arterial pressure.
The primary endothelium-derived vasodilating gas; deficiency implicated in hypertension and heart disease.
Increased local blood flow in response to increased tissue metabolic demand.
The flushing/increased flow seen after a temporary flow reduction is reversed (e.g., after a tourniquet is removed).
A stationary blood clot formed within a vessel or heart chamber.
Material (often a dislodged thrombus) that travels through the bloodstream and lodges elsewhere, obstructing flow.
Sudden arterial smooth muscle constriction causing flow obstruction.
Inflammation of an artery's intima / a vein's lining / an autoimmune arterial inflammatory process, respectively.
Increased pressure within a fascial compartment compromising circulation — from swelling, bleeding, or external compression.
General term for arterial thickening/hardening; encompasses Mönckeberg sclerosis, arteriolar sclerosis, and atherosclerosis.
Plaque buildup (lipid, smooth muscle, inflammatory debris) in the intima of large/medium arteries — the dominant, most clinically significant arteriosclerotic process.
A localized dilation of an arterial wall — a weakened area at risk of rupture.
An abnormal direct communication between an artery and a vein.
Failure of venous valves to close properly, allowing backward blood flow.
Superficial vein valvular incompetence causing dilated, tortuous, visible veins.
Valvular incompetence involving the deep veins of the legs.
A thrombus in a deep vein, most often the legs/pelvis — the most frequent source of pulmonary emboli.
Lymphatic fluid accumulation in the interstitium from obstructed/altered lymph flow — primary (congenital) or secondary (acquired/iatrogenic).
Maintaining internal conditions in a stable state around a narrow "set point," via negative feedback loops.
Claude Bernard's term for the stable internal environment necessary for survival, independent of the external environment.
The dynamic process of achieving stability through change — varying physiologic parameters to match resources to environmental demands.
Anything that throws the body out of allostatic balance (Sapolsky's definition).
The body's effort to restore allostatic balance after a stressor (Sapolsky's definition).
Selye's model of the body's nonspecific response to any stressor, in three stages: alarm, resistance, exhaustion.
The fight-or-flight stage of GAS — hypothalamus activates the SNS and HPA axis in response to a stressor.
The GAS stage where the body mobilizes glucose, fatty acids, and amino acids to manage an ongoing stressor.
The GAS stage where prolonged stress causes glucocorticoid dysregulation, loss of resistance, and "diseases of adaptation."
Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis — CRH → ACTH → cortisol; the slower hormonal arm of the stress response.
The fast-acting arm of the stress response — SNS activation triggering adrenal medulla release of catecholamines.
Released by the hypothalamic paraventricular nucleus (PVN); activates both the SNS and the anterior pituitary (ACTH release).
Adrenocorticotropic hormone, released by the anterior pituitary in response to CRH; stimulates the adrenal cortex to release cortisol.
The primary glucocorticoid; mobilizes glucose/protein/fat stores, suppresses inflammation and immune function, slower onset but longer duration than catecholamines.
The primary mineralocorticoid; promotes sodium reabsorption/potassium excretion in the kidney, expanding fluid volume and raising blood pressure.
The cumulative physiologic "wear and tear" cost of repeated or chronic allostatic responses.
When adaptation mechanisms are overwhelmed or inadequate, producing pathophysiologic states across multiple body systems.
The capacity to adapt successfully to stress/adversity, preventing disease and mitigating allostatic load.
Ineffective, inadequate, or inappropriate change in response to altered circumstances.
A behavioral adaptive response to a stressor; can be functional (health-promoting) or dysfunctional (maladaptive, e.g., overeating, substance use).
The experience of perceiving an inability to cope with a stressor — further escalates catecholamine and glucocorticoid release.
Adapting to a repeated stressor by training the hypothalamus to react less forcefully to it (e.g., no longer noticing airport noise).
An alternative to fight-or-flight, thought to be more common in females, mediated by oxytocin and endogenous opioids — produces a calming, affiliative response to stress.
The field studying interactions among the nervous, endocrine, and immune systems, especially regarding stress and its effects on health.
Subjective difficulty swallowing; can involve trouble initiating a swallow or a "sticking" sensation.
Pain during swallowing; may accompany dysphagia.
The coordinated, wave-like muscle contractions that move contents through the GI tract.
Complete absence of peristaltic activity in the esophagus.
A disorder of esophageal smooth muscle function that impairs peristalsis and LES relaxation.
The muscular ring between the esophagus and stomach that normally prevents reflux.
Medical term for heartburn — a substernal burning sensation from acid reflux.
High-amplitude, uncoordinated esophageal contractions that alternate with normal peristalsis, causing chest pain that can mimic angina.
Diffuse, poorly localized pain from stretching/inflammation of an internal organ.
Sharp, well-localized pain from injury to the abdominal wall or peritoneum.
Pain felt at a site distant from its source, but within the same dermatome/nerve segment.
Subconscious swallowing of air — a normal source of intestinal gas.
Belching; expulsion of swallowed air from the stomach.
Small, infrequent (fewer than 3/week), or difficult bowel movements.
A firm, immovable mass of stool obstructing the lower GI tract, common after chronic constipation.
Increased frequency and fluidity of bowel movements; >200 g stool/24 hr is the objective definition.
Diarrhea from poorly-absorbed solutes pulling water into the bowel lumen.
Diarrhea from toxins that actively stimulate intestinal fluid secretion (e.g., cholera toxin).
Diarrhea from mucus, blood, and protein leaking from inflamed bowel (e.g., IBD).
Ulcerative inflammation of the oral mucosa (lips, cheeks, palate).
Gastroesophageal reflux disease — backflow of gastric contents into the esophagus through the LES.
Replacement of normal esophageal squamous epithelium with columnar tissue from chronic acid exposure; precancerous.
Protrusion of part of the stomach through the diaphragm into the thorax.
A mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction from forceful vomiting, causing bleeding.
Dilated esophageal veins from portal hypertension, usually due to cirrhosis; risk of severe hemorrhage.
Inflammation of the stomach lining; acute (toxins) or chronic (oftenH. pylori).
A bacterium that colonizes the gastric mucosa and is the major cause of chronic gastritis and peptic ulcer disease.
Inflammation of the stomach and small intestine, often infectious ("stomach flu").
Mucosal injury of the esophagus, stomach, or duodenum from acid/pepsin exceeding mucosal defenses.
A functional motility disorder with alternating diarrhea/constipation and cramping, without identifiable structural pathology.
A functional (non-mechanical) obstruction from loss of bowel propulsive activity.
Impaired blood supply to a bowel segment, risking ischemia, gangrene, and perforation.
Twisting of the bowel on itself, causing obstruction and vascular compression.
Telescoping of one bowel segment into an adjacent (usually distal) segment.
Massive dilation of the colon, congenital or acquired.
Congenital absence of autonomic ganglia in a colon segment, preventing normal relaxation and causing megacolon.
Failure of the GI tract to properly absorb or digest one or more dietary nutrients.
Immune-mediated intolerance of gluten causing villus atrophy and malabsorption.
Excess fat in the stool, a sign of fat malabsorption.
Rapid emptying of stomach contents into the small intestine after gastric surgery, causing osmotic fluid shift and rebound hypoglycemia.
Severe malabsorption/diarrhea following surgical removal of large portions of small intestine.
A benign colon growth that is the major precursor lesion for colon cancer.
Sessile = broad, flat base; pedunculated = attached by a narrow stalk.
A staging system for colorectal cancer based on depth of invasion and nodal/metastatic spread.
A distensible sac (~30–50 mL capacity) that concentrates and stores bile, connecting via the cystic duct to the common hepatic duct.
Formed by the union of the cystic and common hepatic ducts; ~3 inches long, runs behind the duodenum, ends at the ampulla of Vater.
The complex structure where the common bile duct and main pancreatic duct terminate together into the duodenum.
A fluid made of water, electrolytes, bile acids, pigment, cholesterol, and phospholipids; aids fat digestion and excretes waste (bilirubin, toxins, cholesterol).
Primary (cholic, chenodeoxycholic) and secondary (deoxycholic, ursodeoxycholic, lithocholic) acids that solubilize lipids in bile.
A cluster formed by hydrophilic/hydrophobic bile salt molecules that surrounds and solubilizes lipids like cholesterol.
A phospholipid secreted in bile that incorporates into micelles to greatly enhance cholesterol solubility.
The recycling of bile acids: absorbed in the terminal ileum, carried by portal circulation back to the liver, and re-secreted into bile (2–3 times/day on average).
The muscular sphincter at the ampulla of Vater that controls bile (and pancreatic juice) flow into the duodenum; contracted during fasting to promote gallbladder filling.
The portion of the pancreas that secretes digestive juice (enzymes and proenzymes) into the intestinal tract via the pancreatic duct — over 1 L/day.
The portion of the pancreas (islets) that secretes insulin, glucagon, and somatostatin into the bloodstream.
Pancreatic exocrine cells arranged in glandular acini that produce digestive juice, draining into the main pancreatic duct.
An inactive enzyme precursor, e.g. trypsinogen, that is only activated after reaching the duodenum — a key safeguard against autodigestion.
A small-intestinal hormone that stimulates pancreatic enzyme release and gallbladder contraction.
A small-intestinal hormone that regulates pancreatic juice (bicarbonate) secretion.
An inflammatory process of the pancreas ranging from mild to life-threatening, caused by premature activation of digestive enzymes and autodigestion of the gland.
The self-destructive process in which prematurely activated pancreatic enzymes digest the pancreas's own tissue.
Pancreatic digestive enzymes; serum levels rise in acute pancreatitis. Lipase is more specific and stays elevated longer.
A distended loop of small bowel near the pancreas seen on plain abdominal x-ray in pancreatitis, from local ileus.
A widely used scoring system to predict the clinical course/severity of acute pancreatitis.
Severe pancreatitis with widespread pancreatic tissue death; high risk of progressing to infected necrosis, with high morbidity/mortality.
A fluid collection in/near the pancreas, often communicating with the pancreatic duct; lacks an epithelial lining (unlike a true cyst) — the most common localized complication of acute pancreatitis.
Usually painless, often massive fluid accumulation from a persistent leak in the main pancreatic duct.
Surgical or endoscopic debridement of dead (necrotic) pancreatic tissue.
The presence of gallstones within the gallbladder; most are cholesterol stones, a minority are pigment stones.
Temporary, self-limited RUQ pain from a stone transiently obstructing the cystic duct, often triggered by a fatty meal; resolves once the stone dislodges or passes.

Inflammation of the gallbladder, usually from a gallstone persistently obstructing the cystic duct (acute calculous cholecystitis); unlike biliary colic, pain is constant and accompanied by systemic signs of inflammation.
Inspiratory arrest when the examiner palpates the right subcostal area during a deep breath — a classic bedside sign of acute cholecystitis.
A gallstone lodged in the common bile duct, causing obstructive jaundice and putting the patient at risk for cholangitis or pancreatitis.
Bacterial infection of the bile ducts, usually from bile stasis behind an obstructing stone; a potentially life-threatening emergency requiring urgent drainage.
Fever/chills, RUQ pain, and jaundice — the classic presentation of acute cholangitis.
Charcot triad plus hypotension and altered mental status — signals severe, decompensating cholangitis.
A palpable, nontender, distended gallbladder in a jaundiced patient — suggests malignant biliary obstruction (e.g., pancreatic head cancer) rather than gallstones.
Irreversible, progressive fibrosis and destruction of pancreatic tissue from repeated inflammatory injury, leading to loss of both exocrine and endocrine function.
Inadequate digestive enzyme output from a damaged pancreas, causing malabsorption, steatorrhea, and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.
Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy — oral enzyme supplements taken with meals to treat exocrine insufficiency.
A tumor marker sometimes elevated in pancreatic cancer, used to support diagnosis and monitor treatment response (not used alone for screening).
The connective tissue capsule covering the liver, itself covered by visceral peritoneum.
The bundle of portal vein, hepatic artery, and bile duct branches that travel together through the liver.
Capillary-like spaces where hepatic artery and portal vein blood mix, lined by endothelial cells and Kupffer cells.
Phagocytic macrophages lining the hepatic sinusoids.
Elevated portal venous pressure from obstruction to blood flow through the liver; a central event in many liver diseases.
Loss of the liver's biochemical/synthetic functions, producing jaundice, bleeding, ascites, encephalopathy, and more.
Brain dysfunction from abnormal protein/ammonia metabolism due to liver failure.
A characteristic breath odor from decreased hepatic detoxification.
Dilated periumbilical veins seen with portal hypertension and collateral blood flow.
Yellow discoloration from elevated bilirubin, due to biliary obstruction or decreased bilirubin synthesis/excretion.
IgM = acute hepatitis A infection; IgG = past infection or vaccination, implying immunity.
Hepatitis B surface antigen; implies active infection, detectable 2–6 weeks post-infection.
Hepatitis B surface antibody; implies resolution of infection and immunity.
Hepatitis Be antigen; implies ongoing infection with high infectivity.
Antibody to hepatitis C antigens; does NOT indicate immunity, and may be falsely positive/negative.
The irreversible end stage of chronic liver injury: diffuse fibrosis surrounding regenerative nodules, with permanently altered blood flow and function.
Autoimmune small-duct biliary cirrhosis, associated with antimitochondrial antibodies.
Autoimmune large-duct biliary disease with recurrent cholangitis and biliary scarring, strongly linked to ulcerative colitis.
Fatty liver with significant inflammation; alcoholic or nonalcoholic (NASH).
Intracellular inclusions seen in hepatocytes in alcoholic hepatitis.
Autosomal recessive HFE gene mutation causing excessive iron absorption and deposition in organs.
Autosomal recessive ATP7B gene mutation causing excessive copper accumulation in the liver and other organs.
Brownish corneal rings seen on slit-lamp exam, characteristic (but not required) for Wilson disease diagnosis.
N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine — the toxic metabolite of acetaminophen that causes hepatic necrosis when glutathione is overwhelmed.
Brain injury (basal ganglia/thalamus staining) from unconjugated bilirubin crossing the immature neonatal blood-brain barrier.
A harmless, self-limited (≤2 weeks) neonatal jaundice from immature bilirubin conjugation/transport.
A pediatric syndrome of fatty liver infiltration and encephalopathy after a viral illness, linked to aspirin use.
Autosomal recessive enzyme-inhibitor deficiency (PiZZ variant) causing liver disease and emphysema.
A very common, benign autosomal dominant condition causing mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
Rare autosomal recessive disorder of absent/reduced bilirubin conjugation; type I is fatal without transplant, type II is milder.
Rare autosomal recessive disorder of bile salt excretion causing severe jaundice, pruritus, and malabsorption.
Autosomal dominant condition with a paucity of intrahepatic bile ducts plus bony/cardiac malformations.
Extrahepatic ductopenia — a congenital or acquired obliteration of the extrahepatic bile ducts; a common serious birth defect.
A hepatoportoenteric connection surgery for biliary atresia; usually a bridge to eventual liver transplant, not curative.
Liver injury from hypotension/reduced perfusion, common in older patients with cardiovascular disease.
Fat-soluble bilirubin bound to albumin, not yet processed by the liver; can cross the blood-brain barrier, cannot be excreted in urine.
Water-soluble bilirubin after hepatocyte processing (glucuronidation); excretable in bile and urine.
Jaundice from excess bilirubin production (usually hemolysis) overwhelming a normal liver's conjugation capacity; unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
Jaundice from damaged hepatocytes unable to properly conjugate and excrete bilirubin; typically a mixed (both unconjugated and conjugated) picture.
Jaundice from blocked bile flow after the liver has already conjugated bilirubin; conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, with dark urine and pale/clay-colored stools.
A colorless bilirubin breakdown product formed by colonic bacteria; most becomes stercobilin (colors stool), some is reabsorbed and excreted in urine.
The clinical measurement used to diagnose portal hypertension; normal is 1–5 mmHg, clinically significant portal hypertension is ≥10 mmHg.
Acute liver failure with rapid-onset hepatic encephalopathy and severe coagulopathy within days to weeks, in a person with no prior chronic liver disease.
Reversible neurologic dysfunction from the liver's failure to clear neurotoxins (especially ammonia) from portal blood.
Markers of hepatitis B viral replication activity; HBeAg-positive indicates high infectivity, seroconversion to anti-HBe suggests lower viral activity.
Undetectable hepatitis C viral RNA 12 weeks after finishing treatment — the standard definition of a hepatitis C cure.
Modern oral hepatitis C medications that directly target viral proteins; cure over 90–95% of patients in as little as 8–12 weeks.
The most common primary liver cancer, arising almost exclusively in the setting of chronic hepatitis B/C infection or cirrhosis of any cause.
A tumor marker sometimes elevated in HCC, used alongside ultrasound for surveillance in high-risk patients — imperfect sensitivity/specificity on its own.
Injury mild/short-lived enough that the cell returns to normal once the stress is removed.
Cellular swelling from water accumulation due to Na+-K+ pump failure — the first manifestation of most reversible injury.
Decreased cell size/function in response to disuse, denervation, ischemia, starvation, lost trophic signals, or persistent injury.
Increased cell mass/functional capacity from a net increase in cellular protein content, without cell division.
Increased cell number via mitotic division, in cells capable of dividing.
Replacement of one differentiated cell type with another, better able to tolerate ongoing injury; fully reversible.
Disorganized cellular appearance (abnormal size/shape/arrangement) — an adaptive effort gone wrong; considered preneoplastic.
Severe dysplasia involving the entire thickness of the epithelium.
Pathologic, messy cell death from external injury — cell rupture, content spillage, and inflammation.
Programmed, "tidy" cell death from intracellular signaling — no rupture, no inflammation; can be normal or pathologic.
The most common necrosis type; denatured proteins, tissue architecture preserved for weeks; typically from ischemia.
Rapid dissolution of dead tissue into liquid debris; classic in the brain or with bacterial abscess formation.
Death of adipose tissue, classically from pancreatitis/trauma; forms calcium soaps (saponification), appears chalky white.
Cheese-like, walled-off necrosis characteristic of tuberculosis-damaged lung tissue.
Necrosis of a large tissue area, usually from major blood supply interruption; dry, wet, or gas subtypes.
Interrupted blood flow to a tissue — injures cells faster than hypoxia alone because it also blocks nutrient delivery and waste removal.
Cellular damage occurring after blood flow is restored to previously ischemic tissue — often causes more damage than the ischemia itself.
Molecules with an unpaired electron (superoxide, peroxide, hydroxyl radicals) that damage membranes, proteins, and DNA.
Protective end-cap of a chromosome that shortens with each cell division, eventually triggering dormancy or death.
The enzyme that rebuilds telomeres — active in germ cells (near-immortality) and, notably, many cancer cells.
The finite number of divisions a cell line can undergo before becoming dormant or dying, tied to telomere shortening.
A protein family linking caloric restriction to longevity — help regulate DNA repair and prevent apoptosis.
Derangement of the normal microbiome, implicated as a contributor to aging and disease.
Death of the entire organism — absence of respiration and heartbeat, with no inflammatory/immune response (unlike localized cell death).
Post-death muscle stiffening from perpetual actin-myosin cross-bridging as ATP depletes and calcium accumulates; appears within ~6 hours.
An age-related "wear and tear" pigment that accumulates in atrophied cells' residual lysosomal vesicles, giving a yellow-brown appearance.
Excessively high or depressed hormone levels (or conditions mimicking them) — the two basic categories of endocrine disease.
Intrinsic malfunction of the hormone-producing target gland itself.
Malfunction of the hypothalamus/pituitary cells that control the target gland.
Malfunction originating in the hypothalamus (upstream of the pituitary).
Hormone secretion from an abnormal tissue site, usually a malignant tumor (e.g., lung tumors secreting ADH).
Normal or elevated hormone levels without normal hormonal action, due to receptor or postreceptor defects.
Anterior pituitary hormone that increases lean body mass, reduces fat, and raises blood glucose; many effects mediated by IGF-1.
Insulin-like growth factor-1, released from the liver in response to GH; mediates most GH effects and is a stable marker of GH secretion.
A slow-growing tumor at the junction of the anterior/posterior pituitary, believed present at birth; a common cause of GH deficiency.
GH excess occurring before epiphyseal closure, causing markedly accelerated linear growth in children.
GH excess in adults (after epiphyseal closure) causing bony/soft tissue overgrowth without increased height.
Thyroid-releasing hormone (hypothalamus) stimulates TSH (pituitary), which stimulates T3/T4 release from the thyroid.
Autoimmune thyroiditis; the most common cause of acquired hypothyroidism.
Significant mental/physical developmental defects from untreated congenital hypothyroidism.
Generalized nonpitting edema from glycosaminoglycan accumulation, seen in severe/prolonged hypothyroidism.
A life-threatening medical emergency from severe untreated hypothyroidism, with altered mental state and thermoregulation problems.
Autoimmune hyperthyroidism from autoantibodies that stimulate TSH receptors, causing diffuse toxic goiter and often exophthalmos.
Protrusion of the eyes from retroorbital tissue swelling in Graves disease, caused by autoimmune injury (not by thyroid hormone levels directly).
Life-threatening acute release of excessive thyroid hormone, with fever, severe tachycardia, and possible heart failure.
The primary glucocorticoid; provides negative feedback on CRH/ACTH release.
Primary adrenocortical insufficiency from destruction of the adrenal cortex.
Acute, life-threatening adrenal insufficiency with vascular collapse.
An enzymatic defect in cortisol synthesis causing adrenal hyperplasia, cortisol deficiency, and androgen excess.
The clinical features of hypercortisolism, regardless of cause.
Hypercortisolism specifically caused by a pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma.
A usually-benign chromaffin tissue tumor (usually in the adrenal medulla) causing excess catecholamine secretion and hypertension.
Increases serum calcium via effects on bone, kidney, and (with vitamin D) intestine; secretion is regulated directly by serum calcium, not the pituitary.
Calcium-sensing receptor — a G protein-coupled receptor that lets parathyroid cells detect serum calcium and regulate PTH gene expression.
Produced by thyroid parafollicular (C) cells; tends to decrease blood calcium by promoting bone formation and inhibiting resorption.
Facial muscle contraction from tapping the facial nerve anterior to the ear — indicates neuromuscular hyperexcitability from hypocalcemia.
Carpal spasm from BP-cuff-induced ischemia — indicates neuromuscular hyperexcitability from hypocalcemia.
Posterior pituitary hormone that increases renal collecting duct/distal tubule water permeability and reabsorption.
Insufficient ADH activity, causing excessive dilute urine loss and hypernatremia.
DI from failure to produce/release ADH from the hypothalamus/posterior pituitary.
DI from renal unresponsiveness to ADH (kidney disease, receptor defects, electrolyte problems, drugs like lithium).
Syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion — excess ADH causes water retention and dilutional hyponatremia.
Tissue-specific glucose transporters. GLUT-1/2/3 are insulin-independent; GLUT-4 (muscle/adipose) is insulin-dependent.
Clusters of hormone-producing pancreatic cells: β cells (insulin), α cells (glucagon), δ cells (somatostatin), F cells (pancreatic polypeptide).
The precursor molecule cleaved into insulin and C-peptide within secretory vesicles.
A peptide hormone cosecreted with insulin by β cells; inhibits gastric emptying, induces satiety, and prevents postprandial glucose spikes.
The enhanced insulin response to oral (vs. IV) glucose, mediated by gut hormones GIP and GLP-1.
Incretin hormones from intestinal cells; stimulate insulin production, promote β-cell proliferation, inhibit apoptosis. GLP-1 also delays gastric emptying and inhibits glucagon.
Production of glycogen from glucose, mainly in liver and muscle, in the fed state.
Breakdown of stored glycogen into glucose, in the fasting state.
Production of new glucose from amino acids and other non-carbohydrate substrates, mainly in the liver.
Breakdown of fat from adipose tissue into free fatty acids, stimulated by declining insulin levels.
Corticosteroids, growth hormone, and catecholamines — all raise blood glucose and oppose insulin's effects.
Fasting plasma glucose 100–125 mg/dL — a prediabetes category.
2-hour OGTT plasma glucose 140–199 mg/dL — a prediabetes category.
Glycosylated hemoglobin; reflects average blood glucose over ~2-3 months. Diabetes: ≥6.5%. Prediabetes: 5.7-6.4%.
Immune-mediated β-cell destruction; the more common form of type 1 diabetes.
Idiopathic β-cell destruction without autoimmune markers or HLA association; rare.
Latent autoimmune diabetes in adults — an autoimmune type 1 subtype typically diagnosed after age 35, often initially misdiagnosed as type 2.
Antibody to glutamic acid decarboxylase 65 — an autoantibody marker of autoimmune (type 1A/LADA) diabetes.
Insufficient insulin production.
The classic triad of diabetes symptoms: excessive thirst, urination, and hunger.
Severe insulin deficiency causing lipolysis, ketoacid production, and metabolic acidosis; primarily a type 1 complication.
Deep, labored, "fruity"-smelling breathing — the respiratory compensation for the metabolic acidosis of DKA.
Severe hyperglycemia with little/no ketosis and striking dehydration; primarily a type 2 complication, especially in older adults.
Early-morning blood glucose rise from increased growth hormone, cortisol, glucagon, and epinephrine release.
Diabetes-accelerated damage to large vessels — cardiovascular disease, stroke, peripheral arterial disease.
Capillary basement membrane thickening leading to retinopathy and nephropathy.
Proteins altered by chronic high glucose exposure, implicated in microvascular complication pathogenesis.
Progressive retinal capillary damage — background, preproliferative, and proliferative stages; the leading cause of new adult blindness in the US.
Glomerulosclerosis from capillary basement membrane thickening; the leading cause of end-stage renal disease in the US.
Small amounts of protein in the urine — the earliest detectable sign of diabetic nephropathy.
Nerve damage from hyperglycemia, divided into autonomic and sensory dysfunction; linked to reduced myoinositol and the polyol pathway.
Degradation of glucose to sorbitol and fructose in nerves under hyperglycemia/insulinopenia, linked to reduced myoinositol and neuropathy.
A biguanide; first-line oral agent for type 2 diabetes — suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis and enhances peripheral glucose uptake without causing hypoglycemia.
Insulin secretagogues used in type 2 diabetes for over 40 years (e.g., glyburide, glipizide, glimepiride).
A newer diabetes drug class (canagliflozin, dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) that blocks renal glucose reabsorption.
A temporary return to normoglycemia without exogenous insulin after type 1 diagnosis, when insulin needs drop below residual production; rarely lasts more than a year.
Brain damage occurring immediately at the moment of injury — largely irreversible once it has happened.
Further neurologic damage developing after the primary injury, progressing over days to years; often the target of clinical intervention.
Oxygenated blood delivery below the level needed to meet tissue metabolic demand.
Cellular oxygen deficiency, from either decreased blood flow (ischemia) or decreased blood oxygenation (hypoxemia).
Uncontrolled cell death with membrane rupture and release of intracellular contents; follows severe ischemic injury (profound ATP loss).
Programmed, energy-requiring cell death in which cells shrink and die without releasing contents; associated with moderate injury (partially maintained ATP).
A substance (like excess glutamate) that overstimulates neurons to the point of causing cell injury.
Glutamate-activated ion channels; AMPA activation causes Na+ entry/depolarization, which removes the Mg2+ block on NMDA channels, allowing damaging Ca2+ influx.
Secondary damage from oxygen free radical formation and inflammatory cell invasion when blood flow is restored after ischemia.
A sensitive indicator of brain function, ranging from full alertness to coma.
A standardized 3–15 point tool scoring eye opening, verbal response, and motor response to assess LOC with high interrater reliability.
Abnormal flexion of the arms/wrists with leg extension (GCS motor score 3) — indicates a lesion above the brainstem.
Arm and leg extension with wrist/leg internal rotation (GCS motor score 2) — indicates a lower, more severe brainstem lesion.
Brainstem reflex adjusting eye position in response to head movement; tested via doll's eyes or cold calorics in unconscious patients.
Brain tissue injury resulting from trauma; not synonymous with head injury (cranial injury doesn't always mean brain injury).
Focal injury at the impact site (coup) or, with polar injury, additional damage at the opposite pole from brain movement within the skull (contrecoup).
Widespread stretching/shearing of axonal white matter from brain rotation/shifting; often causes coma from time of injury.
Brief LOC alteration (<30 min) with no CT evidence of brain damage.
Malignant cerebral edema in an athlete who returns to play before fully recovering from a prior concussion.
Arterial bleeding (often middle meningeal artery) between the skull and dura; classic "lucid interval" then rapid deterioration.
Venous bleeding from torn bridging veins between the dura and arachnoid membrane; slower-forming than epidural.
Bleeding into the CSF-filled space between arachnoid and pia mater; from trauma (bridging veins) or, more often, aneurysm/AVM rupture (arterial).
Bruising behind the ear / bilateral periorbital hematomas — signs of basilar skull fracture.
A cerebrovascular event causing localized CNS infarction; previously "cerebrovascular accident."
Stroke-like symptoms without evidence of infarction on imaging — the definition is now imaging-based, not time-based.
Ischemic but still-viable brain tissue surrounding an infarct core; the target of early reperfusion therapy.
Ischemic stroke from local atherosclerosis/clot formation in cerebral vasculature.
Ischemic stroke from a clot (often cardiac-origin, e.g. atrial fibrillation) traveling to and lodging in a cerebral artery.
Small infarct from occlusion of penetrating arterioles, strongly associated with chronic hypertension and diabetes.
The thrombolytic approved for acute ischemic stroke, given within 3 (up to 4.5 in select patients) hours of symptom onset.
Endovascular clot retrieval for large-vessel occlusion strokes, effective up to 24 hours from onset in select patients.
A dilated, ballooned, structurally weakened segment of a cerebral artery — the most common cause of nontraumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage.
The most common aneurysm shape — round, typically at arterial bifurcations in the circle of Willis.
A grading scale (I–V) for aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage severity, correlating with mortality (15% at grade I up to 95% at grade V).
Pathologic narrowing of vessels near a ruptured aneurysm, typically day 4–14 post-rupture, causing ischemia/infarction.
A congenital fistulous connection between artery and vein without a capillary bed, causing progressively enlarging, hemorrhage-prone vessels.
Progressive neurologic dysfunction from blood being shunted into an AVM and away from normal brain tissue.
Inflammation of the meninges, most commonly bacterial, invading the subarachnoid space.
Classic (but not sensitive) bedside signs of meningeal irritation in bacterial meningitis.
Inflammation of the brain parenchyma itself, usually viral (most commonly HSV-1).
A localized, capsule-forming collection of pus within brain parenchyma, from penetrating injury, contiguous spread, or hematogenous spread.
A transient neurologic event from excessive/abnormal neuronal electrical discharge — a singular event, not a diagnosis.
A chronic, recurrent, enduring predisposition to seizures requiring long-term treatment — distinct from a single seizure.
The area of hyperexcitable, autonomously firing neurons from which a seizure originates.
A syndrome (not a disease) of progressive cognitive decline — memory, personality, language, judgment — from various underlying pathologies.
An acute, fluctuating confusional state with disturbed consciousness and attention; can mimic dementia but has abrupt onset, often worse at night.
Extracellular aggregates of neurotoxic Aβ42 peptide fragments (from APP cleavage) — a cardinal pathologic feature of Alzheimer disease.
Helical filaments of hyperphosphorylated tau protein inside neurons — the other cardinal AD feature, more closely tied to actual cognitive decline than plaques.
The strongest known genetic risk factor for sporadic late-onset Alzheimer disease; linked to inefficient clearance of Aβ42.
The dopamine-producing brain region whose degeneration causes Parkinson disease.
Intraneuronal inclusions composed of α-synuclein; pathognomonic for sporadic Parkinson disease.
Slowness of movement — a core Parkinson disease feature.
Ratchet-like resistance felt when passively moving a Parkinson patient's extremity.
Permanent, nonprogressive damage to the developing brain's upper motor neurons, occurring pre/peri/postnatally.
Abnormal CSF accumulation in the ventricular system from obstruction or impaired resorption.
Increased CSF volume without increased ICP (brain tissue loss offsets it) — classic triad: gait instability, urinary incontinence, dementia.
A chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS causing inflammation and scarring of myelin sheaths.
An electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine into the extremities — a classic MS finding.
Discrete IgG bands in CSF supporting an MS diagnosis.
Defective closure of the neural tube; ranges from occulta (hidden) to cystica (externally protruding sac).
The most severe form of spina bifida cystica — meninges, CSF, AND spinal cord protrude through the defect.
Progressive degeneration of both upper and lower motor neurons causing muscle wasting, paralysis, and eventually respiratory failure.
Temporary (hours to weeks) complete loss of function below a spinal cord injury level, including flaccid paralysis and absent reflexes.
Distributive shock from loss of sympathetic outflow after high (cervical/upper thoracic) SCI — hypotension + bradycardia.
A life-threatening reflex hypertensive crisis in SCI at/above T6, triggered by a stimulus (often bladder/bowel) below the injury level.
An autoimmune, inflammatory demyelinating disease of the peripheral nervous system causing ascending weakness/paralysis.
High CSF protein with normal WBC count — the classic GBS CSF finding.
Muscle incoordination — the hallmark of cerebellar dysfunction.
Idiopathic facial nerve (CN VII) paralysis, now understood to often be viral (HSV/herpes zoster) in origin.
The physiologic mechanisms involved in the pain phenomenon; divided into four stages — transduction, transmission, perception, modulation.
A free nerve ending that transduces noxious (painful) stimuli into neuronal action potentials.
Converting a painful stimulus into a neuronal action potential at the sensory receptor.
Movement of action potentials along neurons from the peripheral receptor to the spinal cord and then to the brain.
What occurs when the brain receives pain signals and interprets them as painful.
The complex mechanism by which synaptic transmission of pain signals is altered — can enhance or suppress the pain signal.
Inflammatory chemical mediators formed from arachidonic acid via cyclooxygenase; sensitize nociceptors, lowering the pain threshold.
The enzyme that converts arachidonic acid into prostaglandins; blocked by NSAIDs.
Myelinated, fast-conducting sensory fibers carrying sharp, stinging, well-localized pain.
Unmyelinated, slow-conducting sensory fibers carrying dull, aching, poorly-localized pain; make up the majority of pain fibers.
Location of the cell bodies of primary sensory pain neurons, just outside the spinal cord.
The pathway by which afferent pain fibers spread up and down 2–3 spinal segments after entering the dorsal horn.
Laminae II and III of the dorsal horn — a key region of multiple synaptic connections where pain signals can be modulated (enhanced or blocked).
A spinal cord region receiving convergent input from both somatic and visceral pain fibers — thought to explain referred pain.
Pain from a visceral organ that is perceived at the body surface, from convergence of visceral and somatic afferents in lamina V.
A key neurotransmitter/neuropeptide involved in synaptic pain transmission in the spinal cord.
The excitatory neurotransmitter carrying the nociceptive message from primary afferent fibers to secondary neurons; binds NMDA receptors.
A postsynaptic glutamate receptor whose activation is thought to induce "synaptic memory" of pain signals.
Sensitization of spinal cord neurons from excessive/repeated C-fiber stimulation, so even mild stimuli become painful — a possible mechanism in chronic pain development.
The main ascending pathway carrying pain/temperature signals from the spinal cord to the thalamus; divided into lateral (pain/temperature) and anterior (crude touch) divisions.
Carries precisely-localized pain/temperature signals to the ventral posterolateral (VPL) thalamic nucleus, then to the primary somatosensory cortex.
Carries poorly-localized pain to intralaminar thalamic nuclei, responsible for the emotional/affective aspects of pain rather than precise location.
The primary cortical region (postcentral gyrus) that processes the sensory-discriminative qualities of pain — location, quality, intensity.
Melzack and Wall's 1965 theory that a "gate" mechanism in the spinal dorsal horn can be closed by non-painful sensory input or descending brain signals, reducing pain transmission.
A midbrain region central to descending pain inhibition; activated by the cortex to suppress incoming pain signals at the dorsal horn.
Receives signals from the PAG and sends output down the spinal cord to activate endogenous opioid release, suppressing pain transmission.
The body's natural pain-relieving peptides — β-endorphins, enkephalins, and dynorphins — acting at μ, δ, and κ receptors.
Pain from actual/threatened tissue damage activating nociceptors; subdivided into somatic (sharp, localized) and visceral (dull, diffuse) pain.
Pain from a lesion or disease of the somatosensory nervous system itself; described as burning, shooting, or electric-shock-like.
Pain from altered nociceptive processing/sensitization without clear evidence of tissue or nerve damage (e.g., fibromyalgia); the newest IASP pain category (2017).
Pain from a specific injury/disease with a predictable trajectory, typically resolving within days to a few months as healing occurs.
Pain persisting beyond the expected healing time, generally defined as lasting 3 months or longer; often involves centralized/maladaptive nervous system changes.
A 0–10 self-report pain intensity scale — the most common adult pain assessment tool.
An observational pain scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) for infants/young children or patients unable to self-report.
A pain assessment mnemonic: Provocation/palliation, Quality, Region/radiation, Severity, Timing.
A stepwise pain management framework moving from non-opioids, to weak opioids, to strong opioids as pain severity increases.
A progressive, noninflammatory disease of diarthrodial (freely movable) joints, especially weight-bearing ones — the most common form of arthritis worldwide.
A freely movable joint (e.g., knee, hip) — the type of joint OA specifically targets.
The smooth tissue covering joint surfaces; its progressive loss is the defining feature of OA. Contains no nociceptors, so cartilage itself cannot directly cause pain.
Cartilage cells that release proteolytic and collagenolytic enzymes after joint injury, starting the matrix breakdown process in OA.
Bone spurs that form at joint margins from new bone formation — a hallmark radiologic and structural finding in OA.
Thickening/hardening of the bone just beneath the cartilage, occurring as cartilage loss progresses in OA.
Bone spurs at the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints — the most common hand deformity in OA.
Bone spurs at the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints — analogous to Heberden nodes but one joint level up.
Outward bowing of the knees ("bow-legged") — can result from cartilage loss in the medial knee compartment.
Inward angling of the knees ("knock-kneed") — can result from cartilage loss in the lateral knee compartment.
A grating/crackling sensation or sound with joint movement — a classic OA physical exam finding.
An aggressive hand-OA subset with central subchondral bone erosion, more joint swelling, and severe mobility loss; tends to affect younger patients (females 40–60).
Fragments of cartilage that break off into the joint space in advanced OA.
A systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease causing symmetric polyarthritis, distinct from OA's localized, mechanical process.
An autoantibody against the Fc region of the body's own IgG; positive in 60–80% of RA patients but doesn't track with disease severity.
An antibody targeting citrullinated peptides; supports an RA diagnosis.
A common 5-amino-acid sequence in certain HLA-DRB1 alleles, associated with higher RA susceptibility.
Hypertrophied, vascularized synovial tissue in RA that invades and destroys cartilage, ligaments, and tendons.
PIP hyperextension with MCP/DIP flexion — a classic RA hand deformity.
PIP flexion with DIP hyperextension, from extensor tendon rupture/laxity — the mirror pattern of swan neck.
Joint disease associated with inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis or Crohn disease).
Joint destruction from loss of proprioception/pain sensation, most often from diabetes, tabes dorsalis, or syringomyelia.
Progressive joint destruction from recurrent hemarthrosis in hemophilia patients.
A disorder of uric acid metabolism causing monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints and tissue.
Elevated serum uric acid — the biochemical precursor to gout, from either overproduction or underexcretion.
Crystalline urate deposits in bone and connective tissue — the hallmark of chronic tophaceous gout.
The symptom-free interval between acute gout attacks, during which urate deposition continues silently.
Cross-linked, thickened collagen bundles from chronic sun exposure, producing a weather-beaten/tanned appearance.
Age-related hemorrhages from increased dermal vascular fragility.
A skin lesion in its original, unmodified appearance.
A skin lesion whose appearance has been modified over time or by external factors (e.g., scratching).
Benign papillomas caused by human papillomavirus, invading only human skin/mucous membranes.
HSV-1 (usually above the waist) and HSV-2 (usually genital); persists latently in the trigeminal or other ganglia after initial infection.
Reactivation of latent varicella zoster virus in a dorsal sensory ganglion, causing a dermatomal vesicular eruption.
Persistent pain (months to years) after a shingles rash resolves — the most important complication in patients over 50.
Dermatophyte fungal infection, named by body location (capitis, corporis, pedis, cruris, etc.).
Dermatophyte infection of the nails, causing white/yellow discoloration and thickening.
Intensified, male-pattern hair growth in females — upper lip, chin, chest, linea alba.
A benign area of selective pigmentation, usually buttocks/sacral area, common in Asian American and African American infants.
A bright-red, raised vascular birthmark that typically resolves by age 5–7 without scarring.
A flat, reddish-purple vascular birthmark, usually face/neck, that does NOT resolve with age.
Small white bumps in newborns from plugged sebaceous/eccrine glands.
A harmless, self-limited scaly scalp condition in infants.
A mild childhood viral illness with major risk only if transmitted transplacentally to a fetus early in gestation (teratogenic).
A viral illness in children under ~4 (usually ~1 year) with high fever followed by a rash — NO lymphadenopathy, unlike rubella.
A more severe communicable viral illness with Koplik spots and a facial-onset rash; risk of otitis media, pneumonia, encephalitis.
Small red spots with a bluish-white center on the buccal mucosa — pathognomonic for measles.
"New growth" — abnormal cellular growth; used loosely as a synonym for tumor.
The term reserved specifically for malignant neoplasms.
Lack of differentiated features in a cancer cell — greater anaplasia correlates with more aggressive malignancy.
Malignant tumor of epithelial origin — accounts for ~90% of human cancers.
Malignant tumor of mesenchymal origin (nerve, bone, muscle, tendon, fat).
A normal cellular gene coding for proteins involved in growth/differentiation/apoptosis/signaling; becomes an oncogene when mutated to gain function.
The mutant, overactive/overexpressed form of a proto-oncogene.
A gene that normally inhibits cell proliferation; loss of function is the most common mutation type in cancer development.
A DNA abnormality that actively contributes to malignant proliferation.
A random, coexisting DNA change that plays no role in tumorigenesis.
An agent/substance capable of inducing cancer.
A substance that causes cancer only in combination with other chemicals/compounds.
The first step of carcinogenesis — mutations that activate proto-oncogenes/inactivate tumor suppressor genes.
The stage where a mutant cell proliferates, often driven by inflammation, hormones, or nutritional factors.
The stage where proliferating cells develop full malignant behavior via accumulating genetic instability.
An enzyme that rebuilds chromosome telomeres, allowing indefinite division — reactivated by most cancer cells to escape normal replicative limits.
Cancer cells' preference for glycolysis (even with oxygen present) over oxidative phosphorylation, despite glycolysis producing far less ATP per glucose.
A key epithelial cell-to-cell adhesion molecule; its loss enables carcinoma cells to detach and invade.
A developmental program hijacked by cancer cells to gain invasive, apoptosis-resistant, stem-cell-like properties.
A small, self-renewing, undifferentiated tumor cell subpopulation implicated in tumor growth, metastasis, and treatment resistance.
New blood vessel formation; tumors can't exceed ~2 mm without it.
Vascular endothelial growth factor — the key signal tumor cells produce (often in response to hypoxia) to trigger angiogenesis.
An immune checkpoint pair — cancer cells express PD-L1 to bind T-cell PD-1 receptors, suppressing cytotoxic T-cell activation and evading immune destruction.
Cancer-related weight loss, muscle wasting, and generalized weakness — affects 60–80% of cancer patients.
A symptom complex at a site distant from the tumor, not explained by direct tumor effects — often from ectopic hormone/cytokine secretion.
Decreased circulating white blood cells — major cause of infection-related morbidity/mortality in cancer patients.
Decreased circulating platelets; <20,000/mm³ is associated with spontaneous hemorrhage risk.
